stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 19+5 weeks gestation with a monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy is referred to a tertiary fetal medicine centre after her routine fortnightly ultrasound. Her previous pregnancy was a term singleton delivery. She has no chronic medical conditions. First-trimester combined screening showed both fetuses with normal nuchal translucency and chorionicity confirmed as monochorionic-diamniotic at 12 weeks.

She has noticed increased abdominal girth over the past week. The fetal medicine consultant has reviewed her in clinic.

data_table.Examination: Uterus equivalent to 26 weeks size, soft, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound deepest vertical pocket of donor twin 1.2 cm (oligohydramnios less than 2 cm), deepest vertical pocket of recipient twin 9.4 cm (polyhydramnios greater than 8 cm); donor bladder visible; both fetuses with normal Doppler waveforms in umbilical arteries, middle cerebral arteries and ductus venosus; no hydrops in either twin; estimated fetal weight discordance 18%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Refer for fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses
options.rationale: This is Quintero stage 2 twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (oligohydramnios in donor with non-visible bladder threshold not yet crossed - actually donor bladder remains visible, so this is stage 1 to 2 transition; with oligo+poly criteria met it qualifies as stage 1 minimum and progresses readily). Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses (Solomon technique) is the standard of care for stage 2 and above before 26 weeks gestation and is now recommended in selected stage 1 cases with progression. Eurofoetus and other trials show improved perinatal survival compared with amnioreduction. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Serial amnioreduction every 1 to 2 weeks until fetal lung maturity
options.rationale: Amnioreduction temporises symptoms in the recipient but does not address the underlying vascular communications and is inferior to laser photocoagulation in published trials (Eurofetus). It is reserved for laser-unsuitable anatomy or post-26-week disease. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Selective fetocide of the donor twin by bipolar cord coagulation
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for severe discordant anomaly, imminent demise of one twin, or salvage when laser has failed. It is not first-line for TTTS at this stage when both fetuses are viable. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Continue fortnightly ultrasound surveillance with no intervention
options.rationale: Surveillance without intervention in established TTTS (poly-oligo sequence with DVPs at the criteria) carries a survival rate as low as 10 to 20% without treatment. Intervention is needed once Quintero stage 1 is established with progression risk. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Plan elective delivery by classical caesarean section at 28 weeks
options.rationale: Delivery at 19 weeks is not viable. Once a viable gestation is reached, delivery may be considered for non-laser-amenable progression but is not the management at 19 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: TTTS arises from unbalanced placental vascular anastomoses in monochorionic-diamniotic twins. Quintero staging: I poly-oligo only, II donor bladder not visible, III abnormal Dopplers (umbilical AEDV/REDV, ductus venosus reversed a-wave), IV hydrops, V demise. Fetoscopic laser is first-line for stage 2 to 4 before 26 weeks (Solomon technique includes equator dichorionisation). Amnioreduction is salvage or for laser-unsuitable cases. Survival without treatment in stage 2 to 3 is 10 to 20%; with laser, dual survival 60 to 70%.
key_points: Quintero staging: I poly-oligo, II donor bladder absent, III Doppler abnormal, IV hydrops, V demise
Fetoscopic laser first-line for stage 2+ before 26 weeks (Solomon technique)
Amnioreduction is salvage, not first-line
explanation.pearls: Always confirm chorionicity in the first trimester (lambda vs T sign); a monochorionic pregnancy needs fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for early TTTS or TAPS detection.

stem:
A 33-year-old G2P0 woman at 32 weeks gestation is referred to a tertiary fetal medicine unit after her morphology scan and follow-up imaging. She conceived via IVF with a fresh embryo transfer. Her first pregnancy ended in a missed miscarriage at 9 weeks. The 20-week morphology scan reported a low-lying placenta with a succenturiate lobe; a follow-up transvaginal scan at 30 weeks confirmed a velamentous cord insertion with a fetal vessel running across the internal cervical os.

She is asymptomatic with no bleeding and good fetal movements. The maternal-fetal medicine consultant has reviewed her in clinic.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no uterine activity, fundal height 30 cm, cervix long and closed on transabdominal assessment
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler confirms a fetal vessel crossing the internal cervical os within 2 cm; placenta no longer praevia (lower edge 4 cm from internal os); estimated fetal weight 50th centile; reactive CTG; FBC, group and hold normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care with delivery at 39 weeks unless labour intervenes
options.rationale: Routine term delivery exposes the fetus to a high risk of vessel rupture once cervical change begins. Vasa praevia carries a 60% fetal mortality if undiagnosed and ruptures, falling to less than 3% with planned prelabour caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Inpatient admission 30-32 weeks with steroids and elective caesarean 34 to 36 weeks
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends inpatient admission from around 30 to 32 weeks, antenatal corticosteroids before 34+6 weeks for fetal lung maturity, and elective caesarean section at 34 to 36 weeks gestation. Inpatient admission allows immediate caesarean if labour or membrane rupture occurs. The trade-off is small prematurity-related morbidity versus catastrophic fetal exsanguination from ruptured fetal vessels. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Plan elective caesarean at 39 weeks with cross-matched blood and neonatal team
options.rationale: Vasa praevia is at substantial risk of vessel rupture before 39 weeks (especially with cervical shortening or early labour). Delivery is brought forward to 34 to 36 weeks to balance prematurity against rupture risk. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Perform an emergency caesarean today because vasa praevia is now confirmed on ultrasound
options.rationale: There is no emergency indication. Antenatal corticosteroids and planned delivery at 34 to 36 weeks offers better neonatal outcomes than immediate delivery at 32 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Outpatient weekly cervical length monitoring with planned vaginal delivery anyway
options.rationale: Vasa praevia is an absolute indication for caesarean section irrespective of cervical length, because rupture of the fetal vessel during labour kills the fetus rapidly. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is unprotected fetal vessels traversing membranes within 2 cm of the internal cervical os, typically from velamentous cord insertion or a succenturiate placental lobe. Diagnosis is by transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler. Management: inpatient admission from 30 to 32 weeks, antenatal corticosteroids before 34+6 weeks, planned caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks. Undiagnosed vasa praevia has 60% fetal mortality with vessel rupture; with planned delivery it falls to under 3%.
key_points: Inpatient admission 30-32 weeks for monitoring
Antenatal corticosteroids before 34+6 weeks
Elective caesarean 34-36 weeks (not 39)
explanation.pearls: IVF and second-trimester low-lying placenta are risk factors for vasa praevia - always evaluate cord insertion with colour Doppler in these patients at the morphology scan.

stem:
A 24-year-old G1P0 woman at 35 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite by ambulance with headache and visual flashing. Her booking blood pressure was 110/68 mmHg. She has had two BP readings at home of 165/108 and 168/110 mmHg this morning. She has had no proteinuria at previous antenatal visits. She has no chronic hypertension or other comorbidities. Her partner is with her.

On arrival she is anxious but oriented. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and intravenous access has been established.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 168/112 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C, fetal heart rate 140 bpm reactive
data_table.Examination: 3+ proteinuria on dipstick, brisk reflexes with 2 beats of clonus, no facial oedema, no abdominal tenderness, CTG reassuring
data_table.Investigations: FBC haemoglobin 124 g/L, platelets 188 x10^9/L (150-400), AST 32 U/L (<35), ALT 28 U/L (<35), creatinine 78 micromol/L (45-90), urate 0.38 mmol/L (<0.36), urine PCR 220 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for acute blood pressure control?

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release 20 mg given by the sublingual route as crisis dose
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in pregnancy because of rapid uncontrolled BP drop and risk of placental hypoperfusion. Oral immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg by swallowed dose is acceptable but sublingual is not. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g bolus then 1 g per hour as the primary BP agent
options.rationale: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis, not blood pressure control. It should be started concurrently in severe pre-eclampsia with neurological symptoms but does not replace an antihypertensive. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus with repeat doses to a maximum 220 mg cumulative
options.rationale: Severe hypertension in pregnancy (BP at or above 160/110 mmHg sustained) is a hypertensive emergency requiring acute BP lowering within 15 to 60 minutes. IV labetalol 20 mg bolus, repeated at 10-minute intervals with escalating doses (40 mg, then 80 mg) to a maximum cumulative 220 mg, is first-line in the SOMANZ/RANZCOG pre-eclampsia guideline. Concurrent IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis and planning for delivery are also required. Target BP is 140-150/80-100 mmHg to avoid overshooting. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Intravenous nitroprusside infusion titrated to a target BP under 140/90 mmHg
options.rationale: Nitroprusside causes cyanide toxicity in the fetus and is contraindicated in pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily as a maintenance antihypertensive agent
options.rationale: Methyldopa is used for ongoing antihypertensive maintenance in pregnancy but its onset is too slow for acute severe hypertension; it does not control BP in the next 15 to 60 minutes. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Severe hypertension in pregnancy (BP at or above 160/110 mmHg) requires acute BP lowering within 15 to 60 minutes. First-line options in Australian practice: IV labetalol (escalating 20-40-80 mg, max 220 mg cumulative) or oral immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg (swallowed, not sublingual), or IV hydralazine. Target 140-150/80-100 mmHg. Concurrent magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (4 g IV bolus then 1 g/hour). Plan for delivery after maternal stabilisation.
key_points: First-line acute BP control: IV labetalol, oral nifedipine, IV hydralazine
Sublingual nifedipine and IV nitroprusside contraindicated
Target BP 140-150/80-100, not aggressive normalisation
explanation.pearls: Magnesium does not lower BP but prevents eclamptic seizure; both BP control and seizure prophylaxis run in parallel in severe pre-eclampsia.

stem:
A 34-year-old G3P3 woman has just had a vaginal delivery of her third child following a syntocinon-augmented labour for slow progress. The placenta delivered intact but soon afterwards she developed brisk fresh PV bleeding. Her uterus felt boggy. She is in the birth suite with the obstetric registrar and anaesthetic registrar at the bedside; the obstetric consultant has been called.

Over the last 25 minutes she has received IV syntocinon infusion, ergometrine 250 mcg IM, intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg, tranexamic acid 1 g IV, bimanual compression and a Bakri balloon inflated to 500 mL. She has bled approximately 2200 mL total and continues to bleed around the balloon at about 200 mL every 5 minutes. She has had 4 units of packed cells, 2 units FFP and 1 platelet pool transfused. Her perineal repair is intact. Theatre is being prepared.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132, BP 92/56 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 97% on 6 L/min via mask, temperature 36.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale, cool peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, uterus firm with Bakri balloon in situ, continued PV bleeding around the balloon, no perineal source
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 78 g/L (post-transfusion, baseline 118), platelets 78 x10^9/L (150-400), fibrinogen 1.6 g/L (>2 intrapartum target), INR 1.6, calcium 0.94 mmol/L ionised (1.16-1.32), lactate 4.2; fibrinogen concentrate 2 g being prepared
data_table.Medications: Syntocinon 40 IU/L infusion, ergometrine 250 mcg IM, carboprost 250 mcg IM, tranexamic acid 1 g IV, packed cells x4, FFP x2, platelets x1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat carboprost 250 micrograms intramuscularly as the fourth uterotonic dose
options.rationale: Carboprost has a maximum total dose of 2 mg over 24 hours and is given at 15 to 90 minute intervals. Additional doses are unlikely to help once first-line uterotonics have failed and the uterus is firm but bleeding continues - the lesion is likely lower-segment atony or a vascular bleed that pharmacology will not control. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Transfuse an additional 4 units of packed cells before any surgical intervention
options.rationale: Ongoing blood loss requires concurrent transfusion AND definitive haemorrhage control. Delaying surgery for further transfusion alone will not stop the bleeding. Massive transfusion protocol continues alongside surgical management. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Insert a second Bakri balloon transvaginally on top of the existing balloon now
options.rationale: Stacking balloons does not increase tamponade effect and risks misplacement. Once balloon tamponade has failed, escalation to surgical or interventional radiological techniques is required. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Transfer to theatre for examination and surgical haemostasis with IR backup if stable
options.rationale: Refractory PPH after stepwise medical, mechanical and tranexamic acid management requires surgical management. RANZCOG recommends transfer to theatre for examination under anaesthesia to confirm no retained tissue or genital tract trauma, then progression through B-Lynch or modified compression suture, bilateral uterine artery or internal iliac ligation, and ultimately peripartum hysterectomy if bleeding continues. In a haemodynamically borderline patient who can be transported safely, uterine artery embolisation by interventional radiology is also a valid intermediate step; in collapse, proceed directly to hysterectomy. The massive transfusion protocol continues alongside. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Commence intravenous recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg as an off-label haemostatic
options.rationale: Recombinant factor VIIa is a last-resort adjunct after correction of fibrinogen above 2 g/L, platelets above 50, pH above 7.2 and temperature above 35 °C. It does not replace definitive surgical haemostasis and carries thromboembolic risk. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Refractory PPH after stepwise medical (uterotonics, tranexamic acid) and mechanical (balloon tamponade) management requires surgical or interventional radiological control. Step ladder: B-Lynch or modified compression sutures, bilateral uterine artery ligation, bilateral internal iliac ligation, peripartum hysterectomy. Uterine artery embolisation is a valid alternative in stable patients. Massive transfusion protocol runs in parallel with replacement of fibrinogen (target above 2 g/L), platelets (above 50), and warmed products.
key_points: Refractory PPH after balloon: theatre for surgical haemostasis OR IR for embolisation if stable
Massive transfusion protocol runs in parallel - replace fibrinogen, platelets, warm products
Recombinant factor VIIa is a last-resort adjunct, not first-line
explanation.pearls: If a multiparous woman bleeds around an apparently well-positioned Bakri balloon, suspect lower-segment or cervical source not covered by intrauterine tamponade - examination under anaesthesia is essential before further escalation.

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with diet-controlled gestational diabetes is in the second stage of labour at 39+5 weeks. Her previous pregnancy resulted in a 4.4 kg infant delivered vaginally without complications. Estimated fetal weight on a growth scan two weeks ago was 4.3 kg. Her labour has progressed normally and the head has just delivered with the chin pointing toward the maternal left thigh; the head retracts against the perineum.

Routine downward traction has failed to deliver the anterior shoulder. The midwife has called for help and the obstetric registrar is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C, fetal heart rate decelerating to 90 bpm
data_table.Examination: Head delivered, turtle sign positive, no anterior shoulder descent with maternal effort and routine traction, mother positioned in semi-recumbent
data_table.Investigations: Not applicable - this is an intrapartum emergency

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Apply suprapubic pressure and pull harder with fundal pressure to dislodge the shoulder
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia because it impacts the anterior shoulder further behind the symphysis pubis and may cause uterine rupture. Suprapubic pressure (Rubin I) is appropriate; fundal pressure is not. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Cut a generous mediolateral episiotomy and continue routine downward traction now
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not address the bony obstruction. It may be performed selectively to allow internal manoeuvres, but is not the first step before McRoberts and suprapubic pressure. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Proceed immediately to deliberate clavicular fracture (cleidotomy) as initial step
options.rationale: Deliberate cleidotomy is a last resort after all other manoeuvres including internal rotation, delivery of posterior arm and all-fours position have failed. It is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Apply Zavanelli manoeuvre by replacing the head and proceed to emergency caesarean
options.rationale: Zavanelli is a last-resort manoeuvre after all standard interventions have failed. It is not the first response. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflex maternal thighs) with suprapubic pressure
options.rationale: RANZCOG/RCOG follow the HELPERR mnemonic - Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal manoeuvres: Rubin II, Woods, reverse Woods), Remove the posterior arm, Roll the woman onto all fours. McRoberts hyperflexion plus suprapubic pressure resolves up to 90% of shoulder dystocia by widening the pelvic outlet and pushing the anterior shoulder into the oblique. Fundal pressure is contraindicated; routine downward traction must be substituted by axial traction only. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is diagnosed when the anterior shoulder fails to deliver with routine downward traction after delivery of the head. HELPERR: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure (suprapubic), Enter (internal manoeuvres), Remove posterior arm, Roll to all fours. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure resolves up to 90%. Avoid fundal pressure (uterine rupture, worsens impaction). Document time-line and which manoeuvres used.
key_points: HELPERR mnemonic structures stepwise response
McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure first - 90% success
Fundal pressure CONTRAINDICATED; axial (not lateral) traction only
explanation.pearls: After every shoulder dystocia document the head-to-body interval, the manoeuvres used, the position of the fetal head at presentation and any newborn injury (brachial plexus, clavicle); this is a high-medico-legal-risk event and contemporaneous notes are critical.

stem:
A 26-year-old G1P0 woman at 35+6 weeks gestation with a twin pregnancy is admitted to the high-risk obstetric ward with a week of malaise, anorexia and nausea, and 3 days of right upper quadrant discomfort. She has been increasingly drowsy over the last 24 hours. She has no past medical history and her pregnancy was uncomplicated until now. Her BP at the last antenatal visit was 118/74 mmHg.

On admission she is jaundiced and sleepy but rousable. Her partner reports she has been confused about names of family members this morning. The obstetric registrar and gastroenterology registrar are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 134/84 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 37.1 °C, fetal heart rates both reactive
data_table.Examination: Sclera icteric, jaundice on skin, mild RUQ tenderness, no peripheral oedema, mild asterixis, BSL 2.4 mmol/L, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: AST 580 U/L (<35), ALT 480 U/L (<35), bilirubin 142 micromol/L (3-20) predominantly conjugated, ALP 280 U/L, GGT 220 U/L, INR 2.4, fibrinogen 1.2 g/L (>2 intrapartum target), platelets 102 x10^9/L (150-400), creatinine 138 micromol/L (45-90), urate 0.46 mmol/L (<0.36), urine PCR 80 mg/mmol, BSL 2.4 mmol/L (low), ammonia 110 micromol/L (<50), white cell count 16 x10^9/L, viral hepatitis screen negative, abdominal ultrasound shows hyperechoic liver with no focal lesion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Stabilise hypoglycaemia and coagulopathy and arrange urgent delivery
options.rationale: This presentation - jaundice, encephalopathy, hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, coagulopathy with low fibrinogen, normal-to-mild AST/ALT elevation relative to disease severity, twin pregnancy at 35+6 weeks and mild proteinuria - meets Swansea criteria for acute fatty liver of pregnancy. AFLP is a microvesicular fatty infiltration linked to defective fetal long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (LCHAD). Delivery is the only definitive treatment and must be expedited regardless of fetal lung maturity; maternal mortality without delivery is high. Stabilise hypoglycaemia (10% dextrose), reverse coagulopathy (FFP, cryoprecipitate to fibrinogen above 1.5 to 2.0 g/L, vitamin K), correct electrolytes and proceed to delivery (vaginal if rapid progress or caesarean if not) within hours. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Commence empirical N-acetylcysteine and admit to intensive care without delivery
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is the treatment for paracetamol-induced hepatotoxicity. There is no history of paracetamol ingestion and the clinical picture is gestational. Without delivery, AFLP progresses to multi-organ failure regardless of medical support. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Transfer for emergency liver transplantation assessment and continue the pregnancy
options.rationale: Liver transplantation may be needed in a small minority of post-delivery patients with progressive liver failure but is not first-line and is irrelevant unless delivery has already happened. Continuing the pregnancy is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Diagnose HELLP syndrome and proceed to delivery after a course of betamethasone
options.rationale: HELLP and AFLP overlap clinically but AFLP is distinguished by hypoglycaemia, encephalopathy, hyperammonaemia, INR elevation out of proportion to platelet drop, and elevated bilirubin out of proportion to AST/ALT. A 24 to 48-hour delay for steroids is dangerous in established AFLP. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

options.text: Treat as fulminant viral hepatitis and isolate while awaiting hepatitis E PCR
options.rationale: Viral hepatitis serology is negative on initial testing. The Swansea features are characteristic of AFLP. Waiting for hepatitis E PCR would delay delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Statements)

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening third-trimester disorder of microvesicular fatty hepatic infiltration linked to fetal LCHAD deficiency. Swansea criteria require 6+ features (vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, raised bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, raised urate, leucocytosis, ascites or bright liver on ultrasound, raised transaminases, raised ammonia, renal impairment, coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy). The only treatment is prompt delivery; mode is determined by maternal status and cervical favourability.
key_points: Swansea criteria distinguish AFLP from HELLP (hypoglycaemia, ammonia, encephalopathy key)
Delivery is the only definitive treatment; do not delay for steroids
Fetal LCHAD deficiency requires postpartum testing and newborn screen
explanation.pearls: Babies of mothers with AFLP should be tested for LCHAD deficiency because the disease can recur in subsequent pregnancies and the affected infant develops hypoketotic hypoglycaemia in the first months of life if undiagnosed.

stem:
A 46-year-old woman is reviewed in the community mental health clinic. She has a 14-year history of recurrent major depressive disorder. Her current episode has been ongoing for 11 months. She has previously had two adequate trials: sertraline 200 mg daily for 8 weeks, and venlafaxine XR 225 mg daily for 10 weeks with no significant response. She is currently 8 weeks into a third trial of escitalopram 20 mg daily plus weekly individual CBT with a clinical psychologist. There has been minimal change in her PHQ-9 score (19 baseline, 17 current).

She has no psychotic features and no suicidal plan. She is keen to add a medication rather than switch again. The psychiatry registrar has just completed a thorough history.

data_table.Examination: PHQ-9 17/27, MoCA 28/30, no psychotic features, no manic symptoms past or present, no thoughts of self-harm, mild psychomotor slowing
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH within normal limits; ECG QTc 410 ms
data_table.Medications: Escitalopram 20 mg daily (week 8), individual CBT weekly

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Increase escitalopram to 30 mg daily as an off-label high-dose monotherapy trial
options.rationale: Escitalopram doses above 20 mg show no greater antidepressant effect and increase QTc prolongation risk. Australian therapeutic guidance limits the dose at 20 mg in adults and 10 mg in over-65s. Dose-escalation is not the next step in established non-response. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add aripiprazole 2 to 5 mg daily as a PBS-listed atypical antipsychotic augmenter
options.rationale: After two adequate antidepressant trials without response, treatment-resistant depression criteria are met and augmentation is recommended. Aripiprazole is the most evidence-supported atypical antipsychotic augmenter in Australia (PBS-listed indication), starting at 2 to 5 mg daily and titrating to 10 mg. It is well-tolerated, has low weight-gain and metabolic burden, and has level 1 evidence. Lithium augmentation is an alternative with strong evidence but more monitoring; quetiapine XR is also used. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg twice daily for the anxious-depressive features experienced
options.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepine use in depression is not recommended; it can worsen depressive cognition, cause dependence and tolerance, and does not have augmentation evidence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch to phenelzine after a 14-day SSRI washout to commence MAOI monotherapy
options.rationale: MAOIs are reserved for atypical or treatment-resistant depression after multiple failures including augmentation. They require dietary restrictions and a longer washout, so are rarely the next step before atypical antipsychotic augmentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) as an alternative to drugs
options.rationale: tDCS has limited evidence in TRD compared with ECT or rTMS, and is not the first-line escalation when augmentation has not been tried. rTMS may be considered if pharmacotherapy fails or is declined. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant depression is defined as failure of two adequate trials of antidepressants from different classes at therapeutic dose for at least 4 to 8 weeks. First-line augmentation options in Australian practice: aripiprazole (PBS-listed, level 1 evidence, 2 to 10 mg), lithium (target 0.4-0.8 mmol/L), or quetiapine XR. ECT is reserved for severe disease, psychotic features, urgent need to respond, or augmentation failure.
key_points: TRD = failure of 2 adequate trials at therapeutic dose for 4-8 weeks
Aripiprazole 2-10 mg is PBS-listed for TRD augmentation
Lithium augmentation alternative; check renal function and thyroid before starting
explanation.pearls: Before declaring TRD, always confirm adherence, rule out occult bipolarity (a positive screen flips the strategy to a mood stabiliser), screen for substance use, and check thyroid function and B12/folate.

subtopic_detail: Lithium chronic toxicity precipitated by ACE inhibitor
stem:
A 58-year-old woman with type 1 bipolar disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate for 14 years. Her most recent lithium level a month ago was 0.7 mmol/L. She has a recent diagnosis of hypertension; her GP started perindopril 5 mg daily 12 days ago. She also takes a topical NSAID gel intermittently for shoulder pain. Today her family bring her to the emergency department because she has been increasingly drowsy, has a fine coarse hand tremor, mild slurred speech, and vomited twice this morning.

She is rousable, oriented to person and place, and cooperates with examination. The ED registrar has just taken a focused history and noted the new ACE inhibitor.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 142/86 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, coarse intentional hand tremor, mild dysarthria, gait ataxic, no focal weakness, no nystagmus, no seizure activity
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 2.1 mmol/L (therapeutic 0.6-1.0), creatinine 132 micromol/L (45-90, baseline 78), sodium 136 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, ECG normal QTc, BSL 5.4
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte (long-term), perindopril 5 mg daily (12 days), topical diclofenac PRN, atorvastatin 20 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Continue lithium and add intravenous frusemide to enhance lithium renal excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretics increase lithium reabsorption proximally by causing volume depletion and aldosterone-driven sodium retention, which paradoxically RAISES lithium levels. Diuretics are contraindicated in lithium toxicity. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Withhold lithium and give 50 mL of 50% dextrose for presumed hypoglycaemic cause
options.rationale: BSL is normal; this is not hypoglycaemic encephalopathy. Withholding lithium is correct but glucose administration is not indicated. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Stop lithium and ACE, give intravenous saline, and discuss haemodialysis with renal
options.rationale: Chronic lithium toxicity at level 2.1 mmol/L with neurological signs (drowsiness, ataxia, coarse tremor, dysarthria), in a patient on long-term lithium and recently started ACE inhibitor with new renal impairment, warrants withholding the offending drugs, restoring volume status carefully with 0.9% saline to enhance renal excretion, and considering haemodialysis. EXTRIP guidance suggests dialysis is indicated when level above 4.0 mmol/L OR above 2.5 mmol/L with neurological symptoms OR with significant renal impairment. Chronic toxicity (vs acute overdose) develops at lower levels because of tissue lithium accumulation. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Continue lithium at half the current dose and review lithium level in 7 days time
options.rationale: Active neurotoxicity requires lithium suspension, not dose halving and outpatient review. The risk of cerebellar permanent injury (syndrome of irreversible lithium-effectuated neurotoxicity, SILENT) is real. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g orally to bind any unabsorbed lithium dose
options.rationale: Lithium is not adsorbed by activated charcoal. Charcoal is ineffective in lithium poisoning. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity is precipitated by drugs that reduce renal clearance: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazide and loop diuretics, NSAIDs, and dehydration from any cause. Chronic toxicity occurs at lower serum levels than acute overdose because tissue stores are saturated. Withhold the lithium, withhold precipitating drugs, maintain volume status with 0.9% saline, and dialyse when level above 4.0 mmol/L OR above 2.5 mmol/L with neurological symptoms OR significant renal impairment. SILENT (syndrome of irreversible lithium-effectuated neurotoxicity) is a feared permanent cerebellar syndrome.
key_points: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, thiazides, NSAIDs raise lithium levels
Chronic toxicity at lower levels because of tissue accumulation
Dialysis indicated above 4.0 mmol/L or above 2.5 mmol/L with symptoms
explanation.pearls: Counsel all patients on lithium that any new prescription, OTC NSAID or dehydration episode is a trigger to recheck the lithium level within a week; the drug-drug-disease triangle here is particularly common.

stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to her GP with her 8-week-old son. She has been increasingly tearful since 2 weeks postpartum. She describes low mood most days, loss of pleasure, poor sleep beyond what is needed by the baby, intrusive guilt about being a bad mother, and intermittent thoughts that her baby might be better off without her. She has no specific suicidal plan and no thoughts of harming the baby. She is exclusively breastfeeding and wants to continue. Her partner is supportive but works full time.

She has no prior psychiatric history and no medication allergies. The community child-health nurse completed an EPDS yesterday.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, slumped posture, slowed speech, mild concentration difficulty, no psychotic features, EPDS 18/30 (positive screen)
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.6 mIU/L, ferritin 28 microgram/L (15-200), B12 280 pmol/L, FBC normal, vitamin D 56 nmol/L
data_table.Medications: Iron and vitamin D supplementation since pregnancy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer for involuntary admission to a mother-baby unit under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Involuntary admission is reserved for acute severe risk to self or baby with refusal of voluntary care. This woman is engaged in care and has no specific suicidal plan or infanticidal ideation. Voluntary, community-based care is appropriate. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Initiate fluoxetine 20 mg daily and advise cessation of breastfeeding immediately
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and an active metabolite (norfluoxetine) that accumulates in breast milk, making it less preferred for breastfeeding initiation; advising weaning is also not indicated. Continuing breastfeeding is supported with most SSRIs. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Initiate diazepam 2 mg twice daily for anxiety symptoms alongside breastfeeding
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line for depression, can accumulate in breast milk, and cause sedation and poor feeding in the infant. Dependence and rebound anxiety on cessation are also concerns. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Initiate sertraline 50 mg daily and refer for CBT or interpersonal psychological therapy
options.rationale: Moderate-severe postnatal depression (EPDS 13+) with functional impairment warrants combined antidepressant plus psychological therapy. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in breastfeeding because of low transfer to breast milk (relative infant dose less than 2%) and extensive safety data. CBT and interpersonal therapy have the strongest evidence for postnatal depression. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Watchful waiting alone with an Edinburgh postnatal depression scale recheck in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Moderate-severe depression with intrusive thoughts and EPDS 18 requires active treatment now. Watchful waiting is appropriate only for mild symptoms (EPDS 10-12) and brief duration. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Postnatal depression with moderate severity (EPDS 13+) requires combined antidepressant and psychological therapy. Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in breastfeeding mothers because of low milk transfer (relative infant dose under 2%) and extensive safety data. Paroxetine is an alternative. Avoid fluoxetine first-line in breastfeeding because of long half-life and active metabolite accumulation. Specifically ask about thoughts of harming the baby - their presence escalates to mother-baby unit admission and urgent psychiatric review.
key_points: EPDS 13+ warrants combined pharmacotherapy and therapy
Sertraline preferred SSRI in breastfeeding (RID <2%)
Always screen for thoughts of harming the baby AND mother
explanation.pearls: Watch for postpartum thyroiditis (transient hyperthyroidism then hypothyroidism) as a mimic of perinatal mood disorder - check TSH at presentation as part of the workup, especially if symptoms include anxiety with tachycardia or fatigue with weight gain.

stem:
A 27-year-old engineering postgraduate is referred to the adult ADHD specialist clinic after a structured assessment by an ADHD-experienced psychiatrist (DIVA-5 plus collateral from a childhood school report). He meets DSM-5 criteria for combined-presentation ADHD with onset of symptoms before age 12. He has trialled 6 months of cognitive behavioural therapy with persistent functional impairment in his postgraduate study and partner relationship. He has no current alcohol or substance use disorder, no cardiovascular disease, no family history of sudden cardiac death, and his last cardiology screen ECG was normal.

He has no history of bipolar disorder, psychosis, or eating disorder. The psychiatrist plans to commence stimulant therapy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: No tics, no chorea, normal cardiac auscultation, normal weight and height, MoCA 30/30
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal sinus rhythm with normal QTc, FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological choice?

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg daily for 7 days then 80 mg daily as a non-stimulant alternative
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant alternative for adults with comorbid substance use disorder, tic disorder, or stimulant failure. It is not first-line where stimulants are appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Modafinil 200 mg in the morning as an off-label wakefulness-promoting alternative
options.rationale: Modafinil is not approved or PBS-listed for ADHD in Australia and is not the first-line agent. It is used off-label only in selected cases of narcolepsy or shift-work sleep disorder. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Clonidine 50 microgram nocte titrated weekly as a non-stimulant alpha-agonist option
options.rationale: Clonidine has supportive evidence for paediatric ADHD with tics or aggression and is sometimes used as an adjunct. It is not first-line monotherapy in an adult. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily as a non-controlled antidepressant agent with ADHD evidence
options.rationale: Bupropion has modest evidence in adult ADHD but is not first-line; it is reserved for patients with substance use or contraindications to stimulants. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg twice daily, transitioning to long-acting
options.rationale: Stimulants (methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine) are first-line for adult ADHD after confirmed diagnosis. Starting at low-dose immediate-release methylphenidate (10 mg twice daily) and converting to a long-acting formulation once tolerated is standard practice in Australia. Cardiovascular screening with BP, HR and ECG is required before initiation. Drivers must be advised about Austroads requirements - any seizure, sedation, or impairment must be reported, and PBS authority prescription rules apply for stimulants. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: First-line pharmacotherapy for adult ADHD after a structured diagnostic assessment is a stimulant - methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine. Initiate at low dose, titrate over 4 to 6 weeks against efficacy and adverse effects (BP, heart rate, sleep, appetite, mood). Cardiovascular screen (BP, HR, ECG when indicated) before starting. Atomoxetine and guanfacine are non-stimulant options for substance-use comorbidity or stimulant intolerance. Driving advice per Austroads applies; private vehicle drivers with adequately treated ADHD can usually drive unconditionally.
key_points: Stimulants (methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) are first-line in adult ADHD
Cardiovascular screen mandatory before starting; recheck BP and HR at follow-up
Austroads driving guidance: notify if impairment, sedation or seizures
explanation.pearls: When initiating a stimulant in an adult, expect a 25 to 30% non-response rate to the first agent; switching to the other class (methylphenidate to lisdexamfetamine or vice versa) recovers most non-responders.
